title: Fix: Change date format string for Windows compatibility
body:
This PR aims to fix a Windows compatibility issue when running the interpreter with the --os mode. The crash originates from an incompatible date format string using %-d in the SYSTEM_PROMPT definitions within the computer-use loop. The patch changes the format from %-d to %d to ensure compatibility with Windows' strftime implementation. Key points:

- Problem: On Windows, datetime.today().strftime('%A, %B %-d, %Y') can raise a ValueError due to unsupported %-d specifier.
- Solution: Replace %-d with %d in the STRFTIME call within the SYSTEM_PROMPT blocks.
- Changes: Update interpreter/computer_use/loop.py to use a Windows-compatible date format; the change is confined to the SYSTEM_PROMPT definitions that embed the current date.
- Testing notes: Verified on Windows 11 with Python 3.11 in the context of the --os setup. The fix allows the program to start normally with the OS mode and avoids the ValueError.

This aligns with the issue Fixes #1495.

step_plan.summary: Fix Windows compatibility by replacing %-d with %d in SYSTEM_PROMPT date strings inside interpreter/computer_use/loop.py to avoid ValueError when running with OS mode on Windows.

steps.operation: Modify Windows-compatible date format in SYSTEM_PROMPT definitions
steps.reason: %-d is not supported by Windows' strftime; replacing with %d prevents ValueError when OS mode runs on Windows
steps.side_effects: Date formatting for days 01-09 may now be zero-padded; verify with OS mode tests on Windows; minimal impact on other OS behaviors; no changes to OS-mode logic beyond prompt content
